question: [File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to a transaction history endpoint and then abruptly close the connection before the response is sent, causing the 'finish' event on res to never fire, leaving inFlightCounter permanently incremented by 1, and over time accumulating many such abandoned requests to make the in-flight counter appear to show a massive backlog of transaction history processing?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the 'finish' event listener is added to res on every request without cleanup, by sending many requests to transaction history endpoints that trigger multiple 'finish' events (e.g., through middleware that calls res.end multiple times), causing the metrics recording block to execute multiple times per request and inflating all counters for transaction history routes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to a transaction history endpoint with a method other than GET (e.g., POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH, OPTIONS, HEAD), causing the 'method' label in requestTotalCounter and durationHistogram to record unusual HTTP methods for transaction history routes, reorganizing the apparent method distribution of transaction history access and potentially triggering security alerts?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request with a custom HTTP method (e.g., 'FAKEMETHOD') to a transaction history endpoint, causing req.method to be recorded as an arbitrary string in the 'method' label of all per-route metrics, creating a new Prometheus time series with an attacker-controlled label value that pollutes the transaction history metrics namespace?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to a transaction history endpoint that results in a 3xx redirect response, and verify whether the status code '3xx' is correctly categorized (it falls outside the 2xx success and 4xx/5xx error branches), causing allRequestCounter to increment but neither allSuccessCounter nor allErrorCounter to increment, creating a discrepancy in aggregate metrics that misrepresents the health of transaction history endpoints?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to a transaction history endpoint that results in a 1xx informational response, and verify whether the status code is correctly handled by the counter logic (it falls outside all branches), causing allRequestCounter to increment without any success/error counter incrementing, creating a gap in aggregate metrics that distorts the apparent success rate of transaction history queries?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to a transaction history endpoint that results in a 204 No Content response, causing res.end to be called with no chunk (chunk is undefined/null), and verify whether getChunkSize(null) correctly returns 0 so that responseSizeHistogram records 0 bytes, or whether there is an edge case that causes an incorrect response size to be recorded for transaction history no-content responses?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getChunkSize()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a response path where res.end is called with a non-Buffer, non-string chunk type (e.g., a Uint8Array or ArrayBuffer) for a transaction history endpoint, causing getChunkSize() to return 0 instead of the actual byte length, resulting in responseSizeHistogram recording 0 bytes for a non-empty transaction history response and distorting response size metrics?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user access the metrics endpoint (metricsPath) when authentication is disabled, read the api_request_duration_milliseconds histogram for transaction history routes, and use the duration distribution to infer the complexity and size of transaction history queries being executed by other users, effectively reorganizing their knowledge of transaction history access patterns without direct access to the data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user access the metrics endpoint when authentication is disabled, read the api_response_size_bytes histogram for transaction history routes (e.g., /api/v1/transactions), and use the response size distribution to infer how many transactions are being returned per query, effectively reconstructing a statistical picture of transaction history volume without directly querying the transaction endpoints?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user access the metrics endpoint when authentication is disabled, read the api_all_request_in_processing_total gauge, and use the in-flight count to time their own transaction history queries to coincide with periods of low server load, effectively using metrics data to optimize their ability to reorganize transaction history queries for maximum impact?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a burst of requests to a specific transaction history endpoint (e.g., /api/v1/transactions?account.id=0.0.1234) causing durationHistogram to record many high-duration samples for that route, which when scraped by Prometheus causes alerting rules based on p99 latency to fire, triggering operator intervention that modifies how transaction history is served to all users?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: toOpenApiPath()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request where the path does not start with apiPrefix ('/api/v1') and req.route is null, causing toOpenApiPath() to prepend apiPrefix + '/' + path (note the extra slash), creating a double-slash path like '/api/v1//transactions' as a Prometheus label, which pollutes the metrics namespace with malformed transaction history route labels?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: toOpenApiPath()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request with a path that already starts with '/api/v1' but contains additional '/api/v1' segments (e.g., '/api/v1/api/v1/transactions'), causing toOpenApiPath() to return the path as-is (since it starts with apiPrefix), creating a misleading label that appears to be a valid transaction history route but is not, polluting metrics with phantom routes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to the metrics endpoint with a URL that has a trailing slash already (e.g., '/swagger/metrics/'), causing normalizedPath to equal metricsPath and triggering the metrics response path, and then verify whether the authentication check can be bypassed by manipulating the URL encoding of the trailing slash (e.g., '%2F')?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to the metrics endpoint without a trailing slash (e.g., '/swagger/metrics'), relying on the normalizedPath logic to add the trailing slash and match metricsPath, and verify whether this normalization creates a bypass opportunity when combined with URL encoding tricks that cause pathname to not end with '/' but still match after normalization?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: initMetrics()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the nodejs_process_cpu_usage_percentage observable gauge callback, which reads process.cpuUsage() and process.hrtime.bigint() on every metrics scrape, by triggering rapid repeated scrapes of the metrics endpoint to cause the CPU usage calculation to reflect attacker-induced load rather than legitimate transaction history processing load, misleading operators about server capacity?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: initMetrics()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger rapid repeated scrapes of the metrics endpoint to cause the previousCpuUsage and previousHrTime closure variables to be updated on each scrape, causing the CPU usage percentage to be calculated over very short intervals that amplify noise, making it appear that transaction history processing is consuming far more CPU than it actually is?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to a transaction history endpoint with a 'content-length' header set to '0x7FFFFFFF' (hexadecimal), causing parseInt('0x7FFFFFFF', 10) to return 0 (since parseInt with base 10 stops at 'x'), and verify whether this causes requestSizeHistogram to record 0 instead of the intended large value, or whether the '|| 0' fallback masks the issue?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to a transaction history endpoint with multiple 'content-length' headers (HTTP header injection), causing req.headers['content-length'] to be an array or a comma-separated string, and verify whether parseInt() correctly handles this or produces NaN/unexpected values that corrupt requestSizeHistogram data for transaction history routes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to a transaction history endpoint with a 'content-length' header set to a floating-point string (e.g., '1234.56'), causing parseInt('1234.56', 10) to return 1234, and verify whether this truncation causes requestSizeHistogram to record an inaccurate request size that misrepresents the actual payload size of transaction history queries?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to a transaction history endpoint with a 'content-length' header set to a very large number exceeding JavaScript's MAX_SAFE_INTEGER, causing parseInt() to return an imprecise value due to floating-point representation, and verify whether this causes requestSizeHistogram to record a corrupted value that distorts transaction history request size metrics?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to a transaction history endpoint that triggers an unhandled promise rejection in the downstream handler, causing the 'finish' event to fire with an unexpected status code (e.g., 500), and verify whether the metrics recording in the 'finish' handler correctly captures this error state or whether the error path causes metrics to be recorded with incorrect labels for the transaction history route?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to a transaction history endpoint that causes the response to be sent via res.json() or res.send() (which internally calls res.end), and verify whether the monkey-patched res.end correctly captures the response size, or whether the internal Express response methods bypass the patch and cause responseSizeHistogram to record 0 for transaction history responses?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to a transaction history endpoint that triggers response compression (if enabled), causing the actual bytes written to res.write/res.end to be compressed, and verify whether responseSizeHistogram records the compressed size or the uncompressed size, creating a discrepancy that misrepresents the actual data volume of transaction history responses?
